chapters.title: The Dream Begins
chapters.narrative: Meera has been doing tailoring work from home for 3 years. Her neighbors love her designs, and she's been saving ₹100-₹200 every week. Now she has ₹5,000 in her savings jar and dreams of starting her own small tailoring business. She could rent a small space in the market for ₹500/month and buy a sewing machine. But she also needs to keep money for emergencies. What should she do?

choices.text: Keep ₹2,000 as emergency fund and use ₹3,000 for business
choices.explanation: Smart! A good entrepreneur always keeps emergency savings. This ₹2,000 protects you if unexpected expenses come.
choices.educationalNote: The golden rule: Never empty your emergency fund for business. A failed month can sink the whole venture if you have no buffer.

choices.explanation: This is risky! If business is slow one month, how will you buy food or pay medicine? What if the sewing machine breaks?
choices.educationalNote: Risk-taking is part of business, but foolish risk destroys dreams. Always keep a cushion for life's uncertainties.

chapters.title: Business is Growing
chapters.narrative: You helped Meera make the smart choice! She rented a small space with ₹1,500 and bought a basic sewing machine for ₹800. By month 3, her business is doing well - she's earning ₹800-₹1000 monthly from tailoring orders. But now she wants to expand. A customer asked if she can do embroidery work too, which would require a ₹2,000 embroidery machine. Her new business income is ₹900/month. What's her next move?

choices.text: Save her business profits for 3 months, then buy the machine (₹2,700 total)
choices.explanation: Excellent! This approach grows sustainable business. She'll have ₹3,600 in 4 months to buy the machine AND keep reinvestment buffer.
choices.educationalNote: Profitable businesses are built with patience. Reinvesting earnings slowly but steadily is how small businesses become successful.

choices.text: Take a loan from the moneylender for ₹2,000 at 10% interest
choices.explanation: Danger alert! 10% monthly interest becomes ₹200/month on top of her ₹900 income. That's 22% of her earnings just for interest!
choices.educationalNote: High-interest loans can destroy small businesses. Always try to grow from your own earnings first before taking debt.

chapters.title: Diversity Pays Off
chapters.narrative: After 4 months of smart saving, Meera has ₹3,600! She buys the embroidery machine for ₹2,000 and keeps ₹1,600 as working capital. Within 2 months, she gets 15+ embroidery orders - double her tailoring clients! Her monthly income is now ₹1,800. She's thinking about hiring her friend Radha as an assistant for ₹400/month. But this will eat into her profits. Should she hire?

choices.text: Yes, hire Radha. With her help, you can take more orders and grow to ₹2,500/month revenue
choices.explanation: Brilliant! By investing ₹400 in Radha, Meera's revenue grows by ₹700. Net gain: ₹300/month. This is smart business thinking.
choices.educationalNote: Investing in people amplifies your reach. Good entrepreneurs multiply themselves through trustworthy team members.

choices.text: No, work alone to keep all ₹1,800 profit
choices.explanation: By staying solo, Meera keeps her ₹1,800. But she's working 70 hours a week and turning customers away. Is  ₹1,800 working 70 hours better than ₹2,100 working 50 hours?
choices.educationalNote: Scaling a business requires delegation. Entrepreneurs who don't delegate get burned out and miss opportunities.

chapters.title: The Mentor Cycle
chapters.narrative: Radha is fantastic! After 6 months working together, Meera realizes Radha has amazing design ideas. Meera's net income is now ₹2,100/month after paying Radha and expenses. She decides to teach Radha all her embroidery skills. This is the beautiful part of SHG life - when successful women help other women become successful too. One year later, Radha decides to start her own embroidery business with Meera's support (lending her ₹1,000 to start). Meera is now earning ₹3,000/month from her own business AND earning respect in the community.

choices.text: Celebrate Meera's success and Radha's future!
choices.explanation: This is the true wealth! When one woman's success helps lift another woman up, that's the SHG spirit come alive.
choices.educationalNote: The most profitable investment isn't always money. It's investing in people and building community wealth.

chapters.title: The Legacy
chapters.narrative:
One year later:
- Meera has a profitable business earning ₹3,000/month
- She's saved ₹12,000 in her savings jar
- She helped start Radha's business
- She trains other women in embroidery through her SHG
- Her daughter now does homework without stress, knowing Mom's income is stable

Meera's journey shows that small, smart, patient decisions compound into life-changing success.

Key lessons:
✅ Always keep emergency funds
✅ Reinvest profits slowly and steadily  
✅ Diversify your skills and income
✅ Invest in people
✅ Help others succeed

Thank you Meera for showing us that dreams come true with discipline!

choices.text: I learned so much from Meera's story!
choices.explanation: You've completed a story that teaches real business fundamentals through an inspiring narrative.
choices.educationalNote: All of Meera's decisions are based on real SHG success stories from rural India.
